name: Joey Aguilar
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Drew Allar
summary: The conventional wisdom says returning to school is almost always the right call. Because the gap between Allar at his best and Allar at his worst is as wide as any quarterback in this class. He needs structure: play-action concepts that move the pocket laterally, RPO packages that simplify his reads to one or two options, and a run game committed enough to keep defenses from teeing off on his processing limitations.

name: Luke Altmyer
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Carson Beck
summary: Here's what you need to understand about Carson Beck - the physical tools have never been the question. Stand him next to any quarterback in this class and he looks the part at 6'4, 220 pounds with legitimate NFL arm strength. Put on the tape and watch him sling it into tight windows, manipulate coverage with his eyes, work through progressions with veteran poise.

name: Jalon Daniels
summary: Daniels is a tricky evaluation because the physical tools flash on tape but the full-season consistency has never been there across his six years at Kansas. He fits best in a spread-based system that moves him around, uses designed runs, and does not ask him to stand in and read three levels from the pocket on every snap. The problem is that between those highlight plays, the film shows a passer who struggles to connect on the intermediate stuff that makes an NFL offense go.

name: Joe Fagnano
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Taylen Green
summary: The combine workout moved the needle on Green. His rushing production at Arkansas was consistently strong, and any system that leans on play-action off zone-read looks can unlock what he does best. As a passer, though, the concerns are hard to dismiss.

name: Haynes King
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Cade Klubnik
summary: Watch Klubnik's 2024 season and you will see a quarterback who looked like he was building toward a top-20 pick. Schematically, Klubnik fits best in a system that leans heavily on play action, boots, and RPOs to simplify his reads and get him throwing on the move to his right, where his accuracy is at its sharpest. He needs structure, a creative play-caller, and a scheme that keeps the defense guessing with run fakes and misdirection to create the layered windows he thrives in.

name: Fernando Mendoza
summary: Mendoza is a very accurate thrower with excellent size, toughness and enough athleticism. He has fast hands, makes good decisions and is accurate on those quick throws. He operates well out of shotgun and leans heavily on the RPO game.

name: Behren Morton
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Garrett Nussmeier
summary: The abdominal injury that wrecked Nussmeier's 2025 season makes evaluation tricky, but the Senior Bowl performance confirmed what the 2024 tape suggested: this kid can spin it. The injury history over his final two years at LSU isn't encouraging, and scouts who saw him in person at the Senior Bowl consistently noted he looks smaller than his listed measurements. He'll need to play in a system that prioritizes quick game concepts, play-action designs, and movement-based passing to minimize the hits he takes.

name: Diego Pavia
summary: The Senior Bowl weigh-in changed everything for Pavia. Whatever goodwill he built with a Heisman runner-up finish and 39 total touchdowns took a serious hit the moment that number went public. The simplified scheme he ran at Vanderbilt, heavy on quick game and predetermined reads, masked a lot of those issues.

name: Cole Payton
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Sawyer Robertson
summary: Robertson is a developmental pocket quarterback whose best football may still be ahead of him, though the path to getting there is narrow. His best fit is a scheme that uses quick-game concepts and play-action to let him operate with a clean first read and a rhythm throw. He is not going to run an RPO-heavy spread and beat you with his legs, but he can extend plays and pick up a first down when needed.

name: Ty Simpson
summary: Simpson is a slightly undersized quarterback with sound mechanics, touch and accuracy. He has quick feet in his setup and marries his feet with his eyes to stay on platform as a passer. He does have some issues when the pocket collapses, as he gets sped up and the ball can take off down the field.

name: Kaytron Allen
summary: Allen is productive with good size and vision but below-average explosiveness. He’s a fluid runner with ideal patience and a natural feel for when to cut off his blocks. A feel for lane development allows him to fit any run scheme, but his lack of burst is likely to constrict the field and limit his ability to find explosive runs.

name: Max Bredeson
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Demond Claiborne
summary: Claiborne is a twitched-up, elusive slasher who can turn minimal gains into explosive runs. While willing to run between the tackles, his lack of size and strength limits his effectiveness in that area. He's creative in tight quarters, using lateral agility and sudden feet to escape trouble.

name: Jonah Coleman
summary: Team captain and productive three-down back. However, he lacks speed as an outside runner and has average burst between the tackles. Coleman has a clear understanding of run-blocking schemes and protection duties.

name: CJ Donaldson
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Rahsul Faison
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Eli Heidenreich
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Roman Hemby
summary: Hard-charging runner with average vision/burst but good decisiveness and the ability to finish through contact. Hemby is wired for hard running inside, but he has the one-cut quickness to succeed on zone carries. He has stop-start quickness to reset the run against penetration but rarely looks to elude tacklers once he hits it downfield.

name: Robert Henry Jr.
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Emmett Johnson
summary: Johnson is a hard-charging, gap-scheme runner. He’s able to help as a pass-catcher and projects as a solid backup best suited for downhill concepts. Johnson’s production and game-by-game consistency stand out despite average size and top-end speed.

name: Jeremiyah Love
summary: Love is a dynamic weapon as a runner and receiver. In pass protection, he is both aware and willing, but he’s best utilized out in the route. On inside runs, he runs high/narrow, but he explodes to and through the hole.

name: Seth McGowan
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jam Miller
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Le'veon Moss
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jadarian Price
summary: Price is a compact running back with terrific vision and balance. In pass protection, Price is quick to identify blitzers and closes the distance before launching into the defender. He has suffered fumble issues (three inside the opposition’s 10-yard line in 2025).

name: Adam Randall
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Desmond Reid
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Nicholas Singleton
summary: Well-built back with explosive speed whose 2025 season was flat after a strong 2024. He has the speed to make defenses pay but displayed a lack of vision and instincts to put himself in position to do so, relative to his teammate Kaytron Allen. Singleton is a linear runner who plays like his brake lines have been cut, affecting his ability to gather and elude tacklers or cut on demand.

name: J'Mari Taylor
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Mike Washington Jr
summary: Washington made stops at three different colleges and showed improvement with each move to a new school. He has breakaway speed in the open field and is capable as an inside/outside runner. He’s not very sudden in tight quarters and is a step slow to see it and go when the hole opens.

name: Noah Whittington
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Aaron Anderson
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Vinny Anthony II
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Chris Bell
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Dillon Bell
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Skyler Bell
summary: Bell's best role at the next level is as a slot receiver or movement piece in a quick-passing offense. The Combine confirmed what the tape already suggested: this is a twitchy, explosive athlete who can change direction and accelerate out of breaks at a high level. The questions are fair, though.

name: Malik Benson
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Germie Bernard
summary: Bernard is a strong, sturdy wideout. His quickness is a weapon off the line and at the top of routes. His speed is good but not elite, and he lacks a home run gear to pull away from pursuit.

name: Denzel Boston
summary: Boston is a big-framed wideout with exceptional ball skills and production. He uses his upper-body strength to power through press coverage. He lines up both outside and in the slot.

name: Zachariah Branch
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Chris Brazzell II
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Barion Brown
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Deion Burks
summary: The Combine answered the one question that still lingered with Burks: just how athletic is he? Those numbers, paired with 26 bench reps at 180 pounds, suggest a receiver who is stronger and more explosive than his frame would indicate. Outside the numbers, against bigger corners who can press and reroute him, the limitations are harder to mask.

name: Jeff Caldwell
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Josh Cameron
summary: Cameron is not going to be the guy who takes the top off a defense or runs away from anyone in the open field. The best fit for Cameron is a timing-based offense that values spacing, play-action crossers, and intermediate route combinations over pure vertical speed. He will earn his money in the red zone, where tight windows and physical corners are the norm and his combination of size, hands, and positioning becomes genuinely dangerous.

name: Kevin Coleman Jr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: KC Concepcion
summary: Concepcion is an undersized receiver with elite burst and inconsistent hands. You can see it right off the snap: He uses a quick jab step and then explodes down the field. His hands have been the biggest issue; he has too many concentration drops.

name: Omar Cooper Jr
summary: Cooper is one of my favorite players to study in this year’s draft. He’s strong, reliable and explosive. He uses his lower-body strength to run through press coverage and he’s a loose/fluid route runner.

name: CJ Daniels
summary: Daniels is the type of receiver who probably won't light up a room during the pre-draft process, but put his tape on and you see a guy who understands how to play the position. He uses his lower body to deceive defenders, changes pace within his stems, and consistently wins out of breaks with quickness rather than raw speed. The concern is always going to be whether his production translates when the competition level rises.

name: Caleb Douglas
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Malachi Fields
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Emmanuel Henderson Jr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Chris Hilton Jr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jordan Hudson
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Ted Hurst
summary: Hurst fits best as a Z receiver in an offense that uses motion and formation variety to manufacture free releases. He changes speeds through his stems, sinks his hips on breaks with fluidity rare for his frame, and finds zone windows with a feel that his average depth of target shift from 17.2 to 12.6 yards between 2024 and 2025 confirms was already developing. There is room for 10 to 15 pounds of added weight without losing the movement skills, and that kind of development directly addresses his two biggest limitations: surviving press and finishing contested catches, where his numbers were only average despite the length.

name: Caullin Lacy
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Bryce Lance
summary: The Combine changed everything for Bryce Lance. Lance tested as one of the most explosive athletes at his position in combine history, and when you pair that kind of verified speed and burst with 6-3 length, a 41.5-inch vertical, and a 62 percent contested catch rate, you are looking at a receiver who can win at all three levels of the field. Before Indianapolis, the scouting conversation centered on a productive FCS receiver who might lack the speed to separate at the NFL level.

name: Ja'kobi Lane
summary: Lane's game is built around the catch point, and that skill translates. His best role at the next level is as a boundary X receiver in a scheme that values isolation routes, play-action shots, and designed red-zone targets. He wins above the rim, owns contested situations, and understands how to use his frame to create advantages in tight coverage.

name: Kendrick Law
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Makai Lemon
summary: Lemon is one of my favorite players in this draft. He is at his best working in the slot. He is a wideout with a running back's body and a linebacker's temperament.

name: Eric McAlister
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Donaven McCulley
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Eric Rivers
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Chase Roberts
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Elijah Sarratt
summary: When you watch Sarratt work, you see a receiver who understands exactly what he is and maximizes every tool in his arsenal. The lack of explosiveness will limit his role in offenses built around vertical shots and after-catch production, but coordinators who want a reliable chain-mover and touchdown scorer with size and toughness will find plenty to like. What he will do is catch everything thrown his way, find the soft spot in zone coverage on third-and-7, and come down with contested balls in the red zone when the quarterback needs a bailout option.

name: De'Zhaun Stribling
summary: Stribling's best reps are the ones where he gets vertical in a hurry and makes corners pay for giving him space. The short-area game still needs refinement, and his hips stay too high on underneath breaks, but the things he does well he does at a high level. When defensive backs play off or bail at the snap, he closes that cushion fast with his long stride and forces them into a recovery they cannot win.

name: J. Michael Sturdivant
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Carnell Tate
summary: Tate is a tall, long wideout with better play speed than timed speed and outstanding production. He has excellent suddenness to defeat press coverage, and he covers ground quickly with his long stride. He attacks the defender's leverage when they play him in off coverage.

name: Zavion Thomas
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Brenen Thompson
summary: Here is what I keep coming back to after watching Thompson: he does one thing at a ridiculous level, and it is the one thing that never stops being valuable in the NFL. He thrives motioned into clean releases, running posts and deep overs off play-action, hunting soft spots in Cover 2 or getting behind single-high safeties. Thompson needs an offense built on timing, free releases, and play-action shots.

name: Jordyn Tyson
summary: Tyson is an explosive receiver with a lot of wow plays littered throughout his tape. He is a very fluid mover, and he incorporates a variety of releases and general creativity into his route-running. Durability was an issue throughout his college career -- that's the only factor keeping him from a higher grade/projection.

name: Reggie Virgil
summary: What you see with Virgil is a tall, fast receiver who can threaten a defense vertically and give a quarterback a reliable target on intermediate routes. He handled a broader route tree at Texas Tech, showed he could win from the boundary consistently, and proved that his MAC production was not a product of weak competition. The deep ball was his bread and butter at Miami (OH), where he averaged nearly 20 yards per catch, and while that number dipped in the Big 12, his overall game got more well-rounded.

name: Harrison Wallace III
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jalen Walthall
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Kaden Wetjen
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Antonio Williams
summary: Williams is a slightly undersized slot receiver. He has outstanding short-area quickness, ball skills and run-after-catch ability. He explodes off the ball with quick, choppy steps, setting up defenders and creating separation out of the break point.

name: Colbie Young
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Rueben Bain Jr.
school: Bain is a thick, square edge rusher with short arms. His game tape is littered with disruption, destruction and dominance. As a pass rusher, he lacks an elite get-off, but he wins with leverage, power and polish. He launches out of his four-point stance, uproots offensive tackles and forces open one of their shoulders to get to the quarterback. He has a nasty chop/rip move, violent hump move and nifty Euro step. Against the run, he ragdolls tight ends and his motor to chase is unrelenting. Overall, he might lack ideal speed and length, but those shortcomings haven't stopped him from taking over big games in key moments. His drafting team is adding a certified junkyard dog, similar to former Eagle Trent Cole.
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Dallen Bentley
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Nate Boerkircher
summary: Boerkircher is a tight end who will earn his paycheck by blowing up linebackers on the perimeter, sealing edges in the run game, and contributing as a core four special teamer from Week 1. None of it came in overwhelming volume, but the tape suggests a player who was consistently underutilized in the passing game throughout his college career. What makes Boerkircher more interesting than a typical blocking tight end is the receiving flashes that showed up at Texas A&M.

name: Josh Cuevas
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Oscar Delp
summary: Watch the Delp's tape and you will see him lined up wide or in the slot far more often than inline next to the tackle. But asking him to hold up consistently as an inline Y against NFL edge rushers right now is not realistic. He is a move tight end, someone you flex out to create favorable matchups against linebackers and slower safeties.

name: Khalil Dinkins
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jack Endries
summary: Endries fits best as an F tight end in a system that values receiving from the position and can live with some blocking limitations early on. But when he is asked to anchor against a defensive end one-on-one or reach a block in space, you see the lack of play strength show up. He bodies up tight coverage, extends away from his frame, and comes down with throws that plenty of tight ends would let go.

name: John Michael Gyllenborg
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Matthew Hibner
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Justin Joly
summary: An offense that moves its tight ends around and features them as receivers is where Joly makes the most sense. He can wall off and compete on effort plays, and his pass protection work was quietly productive in college, but the run game tape shows a player who needs to add functional strength and play with better leverage before any coordinator trusts him on early downs as an inline option. He tracks the ball well downfield, adjusts to off-target throws, and finishes through contact in a way that gives you confidence he can handle tight windows at the NFL level.

name: Will Kacmarek
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jaren Kanak
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Miles Kitselman
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Max Klare
summary: Klare is a pass-catching tight end first, and his NFL value will be defined by how quickly an offense can get creative with his alignments. NFL defensive ends will eat him alive if he cannot add 15 pounds of good weight and learn to strike with his hands inside the frame instead of reaching and grabbing. His feel for route stems and leverage manipulation is advanced for a college tight end, and his ability to line up from multiple spots gives offensive coordinators real flexibility.

name: Marlin Klein
summary: Klein is a fascinating evaluation because the physical tools genuinely pop when you watch him work. A 6'6 tight end who can run the way he does is always going to get attention from NFL personnel departments, and for good reason. The question is whether everything around those tools can catch up in time to matter at the pro level.

name: Tanner Koziol
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: RJ Maryland
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Lake McRee
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Riley Nowakowski
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Eli Raridon
summary: Watch enough tape on Raridon and you see a tight end who can contribute right away in specific packages while developing into something more substantial with proper coaching and strength work. His length and ball skills create real matchup problems attacking the middle of the field against zone coverage, exactly the type of weapon coordinators love deploying on second-and-six to generate explosive plays. Medical evaluation determines everything with this kid, but assuming doctors sign off on those knees, you're getting a tight end who can handle three-down work within a couple years.

name: DJ Rogers
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Sam Roush
summary: Combine testing was a revelation for a 267-pound man. A 38.5-inch vertical (96th percentile) and 10-foot-6 broad jump (96th percentile) are freakish explosion numbers, the best vertical of any tight end above 265 pounds by four and a half inches. His 87 athleticism score ranked third among all tight ends at the Combine, and his 91.7 SPORQ percentile clears the critical 90th-percentile threshold that separates future contributors from roster fillers at the position.

name: Joe Royer
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Kenyon Sadiq
summary: Sadiq is a short, muscled-up tight end with outstanding speed and athleticism. He is very explosive in his release and he’s able to separate vertically on seam/wheel routes. He needs to improve his dependability as a pass catcher.

name: Bauer Sharp
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Eli Stowers
summary: Stowers is not a traditional inline tight end and probably never will be. He fits best in an offense that uses its tight ends as receivers first, one that moves him around the formation and creates space for him to work the middle of the field and down the seam. What makes Stowers a worthwhile investment is that he has only been playing tight end for three seasons, and the growth from year to year has been clear.

name: Michael Trigg
summary: What Trigg does best is create problems in space. He will need time in a weight room and a position coach willing to rebuild his technique from the ground up. Line him up in the slot, motion him across the formation, split him out wide on a linebacker, and he will find ways to get open.

name: Dae'Quan Wright
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Chris Adams
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Austin Barber
summary: Barber's best football will come in a zone-heavy offense that asks him to move, reach, and climb rather than anchor down in phone-booth battles. A move inside would let his grip strength, hand technique, and ability to reach the second level become primary traits rather than secondary ones, and it would shrink the space he has to cover in protection. The question that follows him to the next level is whether he stays at tackle or moves inside to guard.

name: Evan Beerntsen
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Markel Bell
summary: Bell's NFL value lives and dies in pass protection, and the tape says there is real starter-quality ability on that side of the ball. You do not find 6'9, 340-pound humans who can move with even adequate fluidity very often, and Bell's pass blocking production in 2025 puts him in rare company for this draft class. The concern, and it is a legitimate one, is that Bell is essentially half an offensive lineman right now.

name: Chase Bisontis
summary: Bisontis posted a 62.4 PFF grade in 2025 across a career-high 795 snaps after earning marks of 55.2 in 2023 and 70.2 in 2024. He lacks ideal length, but he brings heavy hands, good footwork and plus core strength to the table. He has plenty of nasty when he needs it, too.

name: Jude Bowry
summary: Bowry's athletic testing numbers tell a clear story about where his value lies at the next level. His vertical and broad jump ranked among the best offensive tackles at the combine, and his movement skills on tape back those numbers up. The concerns are just as real.

name: Parker Brailsford
summary: Where Brailsford ends up mattering most is in scheme. His best reps at Alabama came when the offense let him use his feet. The penalty issue ties directly to the length problem.

name: Joshua Braun
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Travis Burke
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jager Burton
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: DJ Campbell
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Fernando Carmona
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Kage Casey
summary: Casey's future at the next level is almost certainly inside at guard. His arms measured 32 3/4 at the Senior Bowl, which is borderline for a tackle and a big part of why defenders consistently get into his frame before he can establish control. Pair that short length with a tendency to short-set, an unreliable outside hand on the arc, and limited recovery speed, and you have a tackle profile that works in the Mountain West but not against NFL-caliber edge rushers week after week.

name: Pat Coogan
summary: Coogan's best football will come in a gap or inside zone scheme where his responsibilities stay between the tackles. His 2025 pass blocking was outstanding, allowing zero sacks and zero hits across over 480 pass-blocking snaps at Indiana. That production, combined with his work anchoring Notre Dame's line during their playoff run the year before, gives you a two-year track record of quality center play against real competition.

name: Anez Cooper
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Dametrious Crownover
summary: Crownover is one of the more fascinating developmental tackles in this class because the physical tools are legitimately rare. The 2024 tape, when he anchored one of the best rushing attacks in the SEC, is the version of Crownover that gets offensive line coaches excited. You do not find many 6-7, 336-pound men with that foot speed and who have the athletic background of a converted tight end.

name: Enrique Cruz Jr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: JC Davis
summary: Davis fits best in a scheme that keeps things relatively simple for him up front. A gap or power-heavy run game would let him use his natural drive strength and finishing ability without asking him to cover too much ground laterally. The concerns are real, though, and they all connect.

name: Garrett DiGiorgio
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Gennings Dunker
summary: Dunker's combination of raw power, grip strength, and sheer nastiness makes him a legitimate tone-setter up front. He doesn't need to cover much grass to be effective, which plays directly into his strengths. The issue is that phone booth needs to be pretty small.

name: Fa'alili Fa'amoe
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Spencer Fano
summary: After starting 11 games at left tackle as a freshman, Fano lined up at right tackle for the last two seasons at Utah. He has excellent strength and quickness. Not all NFL teams will view him as a tackle at the next level because of his lack of length (32 1/8-inch arms), but he does have five-position versatility on the offensive line.

name: Jalen Farmer
summary: Farmer is a physical tone-setter who brings the kind of demeanor and edge that offensive line coaches spend their careers looking for. His best fit is in a downhill, gap-scheme offense that lets him fire off the ball and impose his will in the ground game. The jump in his pass protection was particularly encouraging; he got tighter with his hand timing, cleaned up penalties significantly, and became more disciplined about holding his ground without overextending.

name: Monroe Freeling
summary: Freeling was the full-time starter at left tackle for the Bulldogs last season. In pass protection, he’s quick out of his stance. He needs to gain more core strength to eliminate that problem.

name: Matt Gulbin
summary: What you see with Gulbin is a player who wins with old-school brute strength and sheer stubbornness rather than any kind of athletic edge. But here is the thing: his anchor is among the best in this entire draft class, regardless of position. That kind of immovable base has real value in the league, even if it comes packaged with limited lateral agility.

name: Alex Harkey
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Sam Hecht
summary: Hecht isn't a guy winning with elite athleticism or freakish power. Hecht doesn't have the movement skills to thrive in a heavy outside-zone scheme that asks its center to consistently reach and climb. The concerns are real, though, and they're worth weighing honestly.

name: Alan Herron
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Max Iheanachor
summary: Iheanachor is a raw, talented right tackle. His tape isn’t perfect, but it’s full of clues hinting he could end up being one of the best offensive linemen in this year’s draft class. He is often late to punch, exposing his chest, but he still has the strength to absorb and anchor.

name: Olaivavega Ioane
summary: Ioane was a dominant presence at left guard for the Nittany Lions. He has a thick, proportioned build and plays with outstanding strength/power. Overall, I love Ioane’s temperament, toughness and ability to anchor.

name: Logan Jones
summary: Jones is a high-IQ, feisty center with strong athleticism for zone schemes. He moves well laterally and shows good hand usage, but his outlier-short arm length creates challenges sustaining blocks and handling longer defenders. He has impressive initial quickness and stays firm inside the framework with his hands while his feet rarely stop pressing forward…Jones' age, traits and center-only value could limit his suitor count, but he has the polish to help early with the right fit.

name: Connor Lew
summary: Lew is a technically sound center with excellent leverage, balance and posture. His consistent fundamentals allow him to win positioning battles, though he can struggle against top-end power due to his lighter build. His upside is high given his age and technique.

name: Caleb Lomu
summary: Lomu lined up at left tackle for the Utes. He has an ideal frame with room to add more weight/strength. In pass protection, he has average foot quickness and plays a little upright.

name: Francis Mauigoa
summary: Mauigoa is a big, powerful right tackle prospect. He has average foot quicks out of his stance, but he can bend his knees and anchor against power rushers. He has a massive lower body and plays with a firm base in pass protection.

name: Blake Miller
summary: Miller lined up at right tackle for the Tigers. He has ideal size, length and strength for the position. He can sink his weight and anchor down against power rushers.

name: Micah Morris
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Febechi Nwaiwu
summary: When you watch Nwaiwu's 2025 tape in pass protection, there is real NFL-caliber work happening. His best reps come when he is covered up, working against a shade or a head-up defender where he can fire his hands, clench, and ride the rusher past the pocket. This is a guard who can anchor against power, control the interior of the pocket, and keep his quarterback upright with heavy hands and a nasty grip.

name: Brian Parker II
summary: Parker's future in the NFL is almost certainly on the interior, and that is not a knock. He faced real ACC pass rushers and consistently won his reps through technique, timing, and anticipation. His tape at right tackle is excellent, but the physical profile tells you everything you need to know about where he will settle at the next level.

name: Diego Pounds
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Emmanuel Pregnon
summary: Pregnon took a major leap in 2025, earning an 86.7 PFF grade across a career-high 910 snaps after never dipping below 625 snaps in any season. He'll get beat to first contact but usually reclaims the rep using well-placed hands, a broad base and upper-body power to displace and finish with authority…He'll be an older rookie who projects as a good plug-and-play starter and immediate run-blocking upgrade.. His 88.1 pass-blocking grade ranked seventh among FBS guards, and he allowed just one sack, two hits and two hurries on 471 pass-blocking snaps.

name: Kadyn Proctor
summary: Proctor is an enormous tackle prospect. In pass protection, he has an explosive first step and keeps defenders off his chest. Overall, Proctor will need to manage his weight to stay at tackle, but he has the most upside of any blocker in this draft.

name: Armaj Reed-Adams
summary: Reed-Adams is best suited for a power-oriented rushing attack where he can fire downhill and move people at the point of attack. His run blocking in 2024, when he was anchoring the right side for one of the SEC's best ground games, showed what he's capable of when everything clicks. He has the length and functional strength to hold up inside, and his ability to find his anchor against power rushers gives him a baseline of competence in pass protection.

name: Jaeden Roberts
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Keylan Rutledge
summary: Pro Football Focus:  ()  Rutledge delivered a steady 2025 campaign, earning a 78.4 pass-blocking grade and 76.8 run-blocking grade while not allowing a sack. Rutledge has quickness as a pulling guard to secure blocks on the edge and when climbing to the second level. He surrendered just six total pressures (two hits, four hurries) His career splits suggest a scheme-dependent run blocker, though, with below-average production on zone concepts (8th percentile) compared to gap schemes (39th percentile).

name: Billy Schrauth
summary: What you have with Schrauth is a guard who protects the passer at a legitimately high level and whose technique in that phase of the game is among the cleanest in this class. The pass blocking numbers were outstanding. His 2025 tape, limited as it was to seven games, showed meaningful growth over what he put on film in 2024.

name: Drew Shelton
summary: Shelton's calling card is his movement ability. His frame is on the lighter side for an NFL tackle, and his play strength right now is not where it needs to be. His hand timing and punch accuracy in the passing game are above average, and when he locks on to a rusher, his recovery skills give him a second chance that most linemen do not get.

name: Jake Slaughter
summary: Slaughter is a smart, finesse center who thrives in zone schemes. He is quick off the ball, coordinated in space and effective at the second level. While his athleticism helps offset below-average size and length, he can struggle against NFL-caliber power in one-on-one situations.

name: Beau Stephens
summary: What you see with Stephens is a guard whose 2025 tape is dramatically better than anything he put on film before it. Stephens fits best in a scheme that leans on inside zone and gap concepts where he can use his size, power, and straight-line movement to dominate at the first level. That kind of jump, from a guy who was solidly average as a sophomore and missed most of his junior year, to a first-team All-American and the top-graded power conference guard in the country, is hard to ignore.

name: Logan Taylor
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Caleb Tiernan
summary: Tiernan's pass protection is the foundation of his NFL case. The leg drive is inconsistent, and his frame works against him when defenders get low and play with leverage. The combine numbers backed up what the tape showed about his lower-body power, and they quieted concerns about his athleticism.

name: Keagen Trost
summary: The 2025 Missouri tape is going to get Trost on boards, and it should. He just cannot keep guys wide enough to win those reps consistently. The best fit is in a gap or power-heavy scheme where he can play swing tackle and kick inside to guard when needed.

name: Dillon Wade
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Aamil Wagner
summary: At the next level, Wagner is going to earn his keep with his length and hands in pass protection before anything else. Right tackle in a pass-first scheme that values positioning over power is where he has the best chance to stick, likely after some time as a swing backup learning the speed of the game. But the foot speed and thickness concerns are going to limit his margin for error against NFL-caliber athletes.

name: Carver Willis
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jeremiah Wright
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Trey Zuhn III
summary: Zuhn's pass protection is elite by any measure. The split between his pass blocking and run blocking is one of the wider gaps in this class, with his ground game grading out as average at best in 2025, but the pass-pro ceiling is the trait that gets him on a field. The run blocking needs to improve, and there will be reps where longer defenders create separation he cannot close.

name: Vincent Anthony Jr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: David Bailey
summary: Bailey is an ultra-explosive edge rusher with outstanding production. He eats ground in a hurry and uses a dip/rip move before flattening quickly to the passer. Against the run, he plays with effort and range from the back side but needs to improve his consistency and physicality when holding the point of attack.

name: Rueben Bain
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Cameron Ball
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Caleb Banks
summary: Banks is a tall, long and athletic defensive tackle. He can win with a quick club move or collapse the pocket with power. He’s very disruptive but struggles to finish with production.

name: Jaishawn Barham
summary: Run defense is the calling card here, full stop. The way Barham attacks blocks, fills gaps, and plays downhill is the kind of physicality that translates immediately to the NFL, and four years of consistent production backs that up. Add in above-average straight-line speed and real closing burst, and there is a foundation to work with.

name: Nick Barrett
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Rayshaun Benny
summary: Benny is a run-first defensive tackle whose best football comes between the tackles in traditional two-gap fronts. His rush plan is thin, and he does not generate consistent pressure from the interior. The concern is what happens on passing downs.

name: DeMonte Capehart
summary: Capehart's combine workout changed the conversation around him. No reliable counter move, limited hand technique when his bull rush stalls, and that gap between his run-defense ability and his pass-rush craft is real. He posted one of the fastest 40 times among defensive tackles in the field, paired with a vertical jump near the top of the position group, and his overall Relative Athletic Score placed him among the most gifted interior linemen to test at the combine in nearly four decades.

name: Brandon Cleveland
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Dontae Corleonne
summary: Curry's relentless motor and ability to wreak havoc in the backfield translate directly to the professional level, and his performance grades back up what your eyes tell you. He's not going to run by tackles with elite bend or overpower them with brute strength. The versatility to align in multiple spots and contribute across all three downs gives him real value, though asking him to be a primary edge rusher in a 4-3 scheme feels like a stretch given his physical limitations around the corner.

name: Keyron Crawford
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Caden Curry
summary: Curry's relentless motor and ability to wreak havoc in the backfield translate directly to the professional level, and his performance grades back up what your eyes tell you. He's not going to run by tackles with elite bend or overpower them with brute strength. The versatility to align in multiple spots and contribute across all three downs gives him real value, though asking him to be a primary edge rusher in a 4-3 scheme feels like a stretch given his physical limitations around the corner.

name: Dani Dennis-Sutton
summary: Dennis-Sutton's combine performance changed the calculus on this evaluation. His explosion and agility numbers ranked among the best ever recorded for a defensive end his size, and that kind of athletic profile gives NFL defensive line coaches something real to work with. But his stiff lower half limits consistent speed-to-power conversion around the arc, and NFL tackles with good anchors will force him into technical fights where his counter game is still thin.

name: Zane Durant
summary: The central question with Durant is one every scout has to answer: why didn't the production catch up to the athleticism? Over 1,600 career snaps, the absence of a reliable counter move or consistent hand plan is hard to ignore. But his grading at Penn State sat in the average range for run defense and pass rush across four seasons, his tackling was a real problem for two consecutive years, and his senior year was actually his worst graded campaign.

name: Bryson Eason
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Deven Eastern
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Logan Fano
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Keldric Faulk
summary: Faulk is a versatile defensive lineman with an ideal frame and length. He lacks ideal twitch and explosion, but he’s a very loose, fluid mover. He aligned up and down the front in Auburn’s scheme.

name: Skyler Gill- Howard
summary: Explosive first step out of his stance is legitimately NFL-caliber, consistently beating guards to the punch and creating immediate disruption in both A and B gaps that forces offensive lines into panic mode. Plays with outstanding leverage for his size, keeping his pad level low and using textbook hip and hand technique learned from wrestling to maintain gap integrity and shed blocks at the point of attack. Shows excellent gap discipline and football intelligence, reading plays quickly and flowing to the ball with purpose rather than just crashing recklessly, which led his team in tackles among defensive linemen.

name: George Gumbs Jr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: David Gusta
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Gracen Halton
summary: Halton is an undersized, versatile defensive lineman who wins with quickness, effort and movement skills. His lack of strength can be an issue, but he fits well in multiple or movement-based fronts. ()  Halton might not be the largest in size, but that does not mean he lacks power or strength at the point of attack.

name: Zxavien Harris
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Romello Height
summary: Height profiles as a situational pass rusher who can contribute immediately on third downs while developing the strength and technique to handle a larger workload. That 96.1% pass rush grade is elite by any measure, and his hand timing, bend, and first-step explosiveness all translate to the professional level. The questions are real though, and they center on whether he can ever be more than a 400-500 snap player as a rookie.

name: Cashius Howell
summary: Howell is an undersized edge rusher with outstanding twitch, tenacity and production. He can win early with speed, rush with power or use his instincts to counter when OTs overset outside. He lacks ideal length, but it didn't cause him problems in the games I studied.

name: Aidan Hubbard
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Lee Hunter
summary: Hunter is a thick, wide-bodied defensive tackle. He has outstanding lateral range and will occasionally shoot a gap for a TFL. He was a dominant run defender at Texas Tech and flashed upside as a pass rusher.

name: Quintayvious Hutchins
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Gabe Jacas
summary: There's genuine NFL-caliber ability in Jacas' game, though teams will need to carefully evaluate what they're getting versus what they hope he can become. The wrestling strength that makes him effective also comes with some natural stiffness that shows up in space. That physicality shows up most prominently as a pass rusher, where his power and initial burst can overwhelm tackles, but his lack of a refined pass-rushing plan beyond bull rushing creates obvious limitations against seasoned NFL tackles who've seen every power move imaginable.

name: Darrell Jackson Jr
summary: Jackson's calling card at the next level will be his run defense. Without developing counter techniques or quicker hands at the line of scrimmage, he will struggle to stay on the field in obvious passing situations at the NFL level. The tackling issues that plagued his 2024 season largely cleaned up, which matters for a player who needs to be dependable on early downs.

name: Bobby Jamison-Travis
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Marvin Jones Jr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Joshua Josephs
summary: Watch him play and you see an edge defender whose athletic profile makes coordinators drool. He wins with quickness and length off the snap, and his hand timing has improved markedly over the past two seasons. Here's the deal with Josephs: he's going to need time to develop into a complete three-down player.

name: Tim Keenan III
summary: Keenan fits best as a 1-technique nose tackle in an odd front or a two-gapping interior defender in a system that values space-eating over penetration. The limitations are real, though. His run defense is the clear carrying tool here.

name: Nyjalik Kelly
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Malachi Lawrence
summary: Lawrence has ideal size, length and twitch for his position. He’s at his best rushing from a loose alignment off the edge. He explodes off the line and can rip through the outside arm of the offensive tackle before bursting toward the quarterback.

name: Max Llewellyn
summary: Llewellyn is one of the more interesting later-round gambles in this class because the gap between his best pass-rush reps and his worst run-defense snaps is enormous. When everything clicks, he looks like a legitimate NFL starter: the first step is violent, the spin move is filthy, and he chains rush moves together with a fluidity that most Day 3 edge rushers simply cannot replicate. His 2024 tape, even in limited snaps, was some of the most impressive part-time work from any edge rusher in college football.

name: Anthony Lucas
summary: Lucas is a prospect whose physical profile and year-over-year improvement tell a more interesting story than his counting stats. The frame is real: 6-6 with a seven-foot wingspan, good functional movement, and enough weight to hold up on early downs. Wide-nine defenses or teams needing a pure speed rusher should look elsewhere.

name: Jackie Marshall
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Chris McClellan
summary: Look at the grades and the picture gets real clear, real fast. In an even-front defense with a good nose tackle role to fill, he can be a useful early-down player who takes on double-teams, frees up linebackers and keeps the run game honest. That gap isn't a minor issue you coach away in a few weeks -- it goes to his pad level and his first step, and both of those are stubborn problems in grown men.

name: Kayden McDonald
summary: McDonald has excellent size and strength for the DT position. He’s at his best against the run. As a pass rusher, he flashes some pocket push but lacks twitch and polish to consistently win.

name: Akheem Mesidor
summary: Mesidor was a highly productive edge rusher for the Hurricanes. He has an ideal frame, relentless motor and pass-rush instincts. Against the pass, his game is more about power than pure speed.

name: Christen Miller
summary: Miller has a strong frame with good length and rare twitch for an interior defender. His quick first step and leverage make him a reliable run defender, and his processing ability adds to his projection, though he must continue to develop his anchor. He has good stack-and-shed against single blocks but lacks the prototypical mass of a space-eater… He'll continue to bulk up and should become a good starter who is more consistent than dominant along the interior.

name: Derrick Moore
summary: Moore's pass rush ability is the reason he will get drafted, and the senior season tape backs it up. He can rush from a three-point stance or stand up on the edge without losing effectiveness. That inconsistency looks more like an approach issue than a physical one, which means the right staff can address it.

name: Trey Moore
summary: Moore fits best as a sub-package edge rusher in a 3-4 scheme, the kind of player you send after the quarterback on passing downs and maybe ask to carry a flat zone on occasion. The worry is whether his best tape came against overmatched competition. The 2025 coverage tape is also a concern.

name: Tyler Onyedim
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Domonique Orange
summary: Pro Football Focus:  ()  Orange is a massive defensive line prospect with elite power potential, supported by impressive weight-room strength. His first-step explosiveness allows him to generate strong speed-to-power as a bull rusher, particularly in attack mode…He projects best as a high-effort, power-based lineman in a 3-4 scheme. His reaction time makes it hard to reach-block him in zone and he has the anchor to play landlord over the A-gaps against double-teams.

name: LT Overton
summary: Overton profiles as a day two selection who earns a rotational role immediately and develops into a starter with time. His best fit is in a multiple front that kicks him inside on passing downs where his power and length create problems for guards. The 2025 regression needs context.

name: TJ Parker
summary: Parker is an intriguing edge rusher with the versatility to slide inside. He doesn’t have an elite burst when rushing outside, but his quickness plays better when he rushes inside. He had a monster 2024 campaign (11 sacks) but saw his production drop off this past season (five sacks).

name: Patrick Payton
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Kaleb Proctor
summary: Proctor's calling card at the next level is obvious: he can rush the passer from the interior. That pass rush ability is not just good for an FCS player, it is genuinely rare. The concerns are just as clear, though, and they are the kind that will define his NFL ceiling.

name: Jack Pyburn
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Albert Regis
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Mason Reiger
summary: After spending a lot of time with Reiger's film, the conviction here is that this is a legitimate NFL edge rusher, but with some clear guardrails on his projection. The 45 pressures he racked up at Wisconsin, plus the Shrine Bowl destruction, tell a consistent story about a guy who can get after quarterbacks. The concern, though, is everything else.

name: Tyreak Sapp
summary: Despite lacking the elite bend and explosiveness that defines premier edge rushers, Sapp's technical refinement and power at the point of attack make him an immediate contributor on early downs. His exceptional run defense jumps off the tape - shocking power from his hands and natural leverage consistently reset the line of scrimmage. When rushing the passer, athletic limitations become more apparent, relying more on power and timing than the quick-twitch traits coveted in high-end rushers.

name: Gary Smith III
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: R Mason Thomas
summary: Thomas is an instinctive and physical edge defender. He lacks ideal height/length/bulk for the position, but he is a difference-maker. As a pass rusher, he covers a lot of ground with his first three steps and has multiple ways to win.

name: Nadame Tucker
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Damonic Williams
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Wesley Williams
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Peter Woods
summary: Woods is a thick, square DT prospect with fabulous quickness and violent hands. He uses his leverage and lower-body strength to absorb blocks and hold his ground. He does struggle when facing down blocks, as he can get wiped down the line of scrimmage.

name: Zion Young
summary: Young is a big, powerful rusher with excellent length and competitiveness. He destroys tight ends assigned to block him. He’s a little bit of a herky-jerky mover, but he has heavy hands to jolt blockers and create knock-back pop.

name: Mikail Kamara
summary: Kamara's path to NFL snaps runs through sub-packages and designated pass rush roles. Asking him to anchor against the run on early downs or drop into coverage will expose his limitations quickly. The concerns are real, though.

name: CJ Allen
summary: Allen is a productive, intelligent playmaker. Against the run, he has good instincts/awareness. He’s an explosive striker on ball-carriers with a high batting average as a tackler.

name: Lander Burton
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Wesley Bissainthe
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Bryce Boettcher
summary: Boettcher's best path to an NFL roster runs through his coverage ability and special teams value. Those traits give him a role in sub-packages and as a coverage-capable backup who can step in without creating obvious mismatches. His zone awareness is solid, he tracks the ball naturally, and he can handle short-to-intermediate assignments against tight ends and backs.

name: Kendal Daniels
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Kaleb Elarms-Orr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Keyshaun Elliott
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Aiden Fisher
summary: Fisher's path to a roster will run through his ability to be a dependable early-down run defender and a quality special teams contributor. His instincts against the run are real. That run defense ability, paired with his leadership and communication, gives him a floor as a backup MIKE in a scheme that keeps him in the box and limits his coverage exposure.

name: Eric Gentry
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jake Golday
summary: Golday is a big, explosive linebacker with a game best suited for work near the line of scrimmage. He also shows the strength to stack, compress gaps and finish with authority. While his performance at the point of attack stands out, he can be a little late diagnosing play design and needs to work with a more preemptive downhill trigger at times.

name: Owen Heinecke
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Anthony Hill Jr
summary: Hill is a fluid mover with outstanding size and speed. Against the run, he isn’t physical to take on blockers, but he has excellent quickness and change of direction to work around them or avoid them altogether. He has the agility to match up with the athletic tight ends he’ll face at the next level.

name: Justin Jefferson
summary: Jefferson is a classic modern-day WILL linebacker whose calling card is his ability to run and cover. At 227 pounds with 4.4 speed, he is built more like a safety than a traditional thumper, and his NFL future depends on a scheme that lets him do what he does best rather than asking him to stack and shed 300-pound linemen on early downs. The concern with Jefferson is straightforward: can he hold up physically at the point of attack?

name: Jack Kelly
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Deontae Lawson
summary: Earned 2nd-Team All-SEC honors in 2025 after leading Alabama with 89 tackles in 15 starts while serving as a team captain. Deontae Lawson earned 2nd-Team All-SEC honors in 2025 after leading Alabama with 89 tackles in 15 starts while serving as a team captain.

name: Kyle Louis
summary: Undersized linebacker/box safety who had more production and made fewer mental mistakes in 2024. Louis has the speed, athleticism and recognition to quickly find the football. He struggles to take on blocks and leverage his gap in the run game.

name: Red Murdock
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Namdi Obiazor
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Harold Perkins Jr
summary: Slightly undersized off-ball linebacker with solid 2025 production but less in-game impact than he showed in 2022 and 2023 before his ACL tear in 2024. He’s adequate in block take-ons and finishes tackles near the line with good physicality. He can still run but lacks his previous explosiveness in close-outs and pursuit.

name: Arvell Reese
summary: Reese is a fluid and explosive athlete. He projects best as an edge rusher at the next level. He lacks a polished rush plan, and there are times when he’s a little late off the snap.

name: Jacob Rodriguez
summary: Rodriguez is an off-ball linebacker with outstanding instincts, playmaking ability and intangibles. In pass coverage, he’s best utilized as a zone dropper. In man coverage, he does have some issues when he’s asked to stop/start and mirror.

name: Jimmy Rolder
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Karson Sharar
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Xavien Sorey Jr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Sonny Styles
summary: Styles is a tall, long and rangy linebacker prospect. In the run game, he’s quick to key, read and fill to make tackles. He can carry slot receivers down the seam and smoothly mirrors tight ends all over the field.

name: Josiah Trotter
summary: Ascending inside linebacker with NFL bloodlines and alpha energy. Trotter’s heavy hands allow him to fit and choke off gaps, but he’s inconsistent in doing so. Block collisions need to be balanced with efficient play-arounds to create more tackle tries.

name: Scooby Williams
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Wade Woodaz
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Taurean York
summary: Kamara's path to NFL snaps runs through sub-packages and designated pass rush roles. Asking him to anchor against the run on early downs or drop into coverage will expose his limitations quickly. The concerns are real, though.

name: Keith Abney II
summary: Feisty boundary corner with the makeup to slide inside and become a problem for offenses as a pro nickelback. He pairs impressive instincts and recognition with the ball skills of a receiver. His top-end speed and closing quickness from depth are average.

name: Marcus Allen
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jadon Canady
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Brandon Cisse
summary: Cisse is a fast, twitchy cover corner. He is a loose, fluid athlete. He gave up some plays in the games I studied, but it appeared to be more of a focus issue than any physical limitation.

name: Bud Clark
summary: A sixth-year senior, Clark’s traits won’t pop but his tape most certainly will. He plays with an overriding desire to do everything all at once – which produces spectacular takeaways and occasional breakdowns – but the good outweighs the bad. He triggers downhill in run support and tackles with proper technique, but a lack of size could lead to broken tackles/durability concerns.

name: Tacario Davis
summary: The Combine changed things for Tacario Davis, and it would be dishonest to pretend otherwise. He is at his best when he can get hands on at the snap and dictate terms early in the route. The inconsistency in his coverage tape over the last two years is real, and whoever drafts him needs to commit to cleaning up his footwork and breaking the grabbing habit before it costs them on Sundays.

name: Mansoor Delane
summary: Delane is one of the most consistent players in this draft class. He makes everything look easy in coverage as a corner who is extremely loose and fluid in his change of direction. He goes long stretches without getting challenged because of his tight coverage.

name: Charles Demmings
summary: Demmings has the frame and movement skills to compete for a role as a boundary corner, and his best fit is a scheme that lets him press and play tight man coverage on the outside. His hip fluidity keeps him in phase through the route stem, and he has the speed to carry vertical routes without giving up easy separation. He will need time to improve his off-coverage reads, but in a press-heavy system he can contribute early.

name: Thaddeus Dixon
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Caleb Downs
summary: Downs is a versatile safety prospect with outstanding instincts and intangibles. In the passing game, he is a smooth mover and utilizes his eyes/instincts to close and limit windows. He's physical and has enough speed to mirror tight ends in man coverage.

name: Daylen Everette
summary: Everette's best football at the next level will come in a zone-heavy scheme. He reads route distributions well enough to pattern match in zone, and his instincts breaking downhill give him a chance to make plays. The concerns are workable but real.

name: Bishop Fitzgerald
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Andre Fuller
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jaylon Guibeau
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: TJ Hall
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Ahmari Harvey
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: AJ Haulcy
summary: Haulcy, aka Mr. His pattern recognition and instincts are top-notch and he’s decisive in his reactions. A lack of sustained top-end speed could create mismatches in man coverage.

name: Colton Hood
summary: Hood has average size but excellent play speed and toughness. He is fluid to turn, open up and mirror underneath. In press, he is patient, staying on balance before using his hands to redirect at the line of scrimmage.

name: Jalen Huskey
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Davidson Igbinosun
summary: Igbinosun is an iron man with 53 career starts, fitting the mold of a press corner with good size and long arms. He uses heavy hands and strength to reroute receivers, staying tight on most vertical routes. However, hip tightness, disjointed footwork and average route recognition make pattern-matching a chore in larger spaces or in off-man coverage.

name: Domani Jackson
summary: Jackson has the physical profile to play outside corner in the NFL. Asking him to play man on an island against polished NFL wideouts right away, though, is probably setting him up to struggle. The length, the speed, and the willingness to get physical at the line of scrimmage all translate.

name: Chris Johnson
summary: Johnson has excellent height, bulk and movement skills for his position. In press coverage, he doesn’t use his hands to reroute the receiver, but he has very quick feet and he’s fluid to open up/run. He will mix in some bail technique, where he’ll turn his back toward the sideline, run and stay on top of wideouts.

name: Dalton Johnson
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jalon Kilgore
summary: Kilgore is a big, long defensive back with nickelback or down safety value depending on scheme fit. He's played an overhang Star role, covering slot receivers and supporting the run, but he’ll be at his best in the pros covering tight ends and containing run games near the box. Missed tackles need to be cleaned up but his wingspan, agility and play strength should keep his tackle totals high from year to year.

name: Will Lee III
summary: The Combine told two different stories about Lee. The run defense needs real work before he can be trusted on early downs. The forty confirmed what evaluators suspected: he does not have the long speed to run with true burners down the field.

name: Hezekiah Masses
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Jermod McCoy
summary: McCoy had an outstanding 2024 season but missed the entire '25 campaign due to an ACL tear. He is at his best in press coverage, effectively landing his one-hand jam while maintaining balance to stay on the hip and mirror. He is a capable tackler in space, but he lacks the physicality and effort to be a force player against the run.

name: Latrell McCutchin Sr.
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Emmanuel McNeil-Warren
summary: McNeil-Warren is a tall, long safety prospect with fantastic production. He’s a very explosive player. He’s a fun player to study.

name: Devin Moore
summary: Moore's combination of length, coverage versatility, and run support gives a defensive coordinator real options. He fits best in a scheme that leans on Cover 3 or Cover 1 and wants a long, physical corner on the boundary who can press at the line and carry vertical routes without needing consistent safety help over the top. The concern is whether the body will cooperate.

name: Louis Moore
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Ahmaad Moses
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Malik Muhammad
summary: Muhammad's combine performance answered the one lingering question about his candidacy: how the athleticism would test in a controlled setting. A 4.42 forty with the best 10-yard split in the cornerback group confirmed the burst and acceleration visible on his Texas tape. He is comfortable operating as a boundary corner, and the arm length should ease concerns about his frame holding up outside.

name: Julian Neal
summary: Neal's best football is going to come in a defense that plays a lot of zone. When the field is divided and he can read the quarterback's eyes within his landmark, his length and instincts show up. In man assignments, the average recovery speed becomes a problem against quicker receivers who can stack him vertically.

name: Xavier Nwankpa
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: VJ Payne
summary: Long and rangy, Payne is a durable 42-game starter and team captain who can play inside the box, over the slot and as a split safety. He reads and responds with good timing from depth and uses his length to smother the catch point in man coverage when matched inside. Payne held his own against three of the Big 12’s best receiving tight ends in 2025.

name: D'Angelo Ponds
summary: Ponds is a productive perimeter cornerback trapped in a smaller body, but he’s not lacking in confidence or coverage tenacity. He matches press releases with good slide quickness and has the speed to stay in-phase as routes travel vertically. Size limitations will likely push him to nickelback, where mismatches against bigger bodies and physical challenges from run games will test his playmaking/durability.

name: Toriano Pride Jr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Ephesians Prysock
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Kamari Ramsey
summary: Versatile safety prospect with solid size. He plays with average eyes in space and good break anticipation when matched up in man. He needs to close downhill in run support with greater urgency when playing from depth.

name: Chandler Rivers
summary: Rivers fits best as an inside cornerback at the next level. His frame and arm length will make it tough to hold up consistently on the boundary against NFL receivers, but his short-area quickness, route recognition, and ability to handle zone exchanges all translate well to the slot. The question is whether 2024 was the ceiling or 2025 was the floor.

name: Keionte Scott
summary: Scott is a dynamic nickelback. In underneath coverage, he has excellent eyes, quick feet and ball skills. He’s an excellent force player, blitzer and energy provider.

name: DeShon Singleton
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Avery Smith
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Genesis Smith
summary: Smith offers ballhawking coverage qualities but has alarming issues as an open-field tackler. He has average top-end speed but good route recognition and premium ball skills. He’s instinctive, with the eyes to digest route development while reading the quarterback like a poker player looking for tells.

name: Robert Spears-Jennings
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Treydan Stukes
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Lorenzo Styles Jr
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Michael Taaffe
summary: Taaffe is a coverage safety whose processing speed and instincts in zone are well ahead of where most college defensive backs operate. His best role is as a deep-half or single-high safety who rotates down into nickel looks against spread formations. He can match tight ends and slot receivers underneath, and his pre-snap communication makes any secondary around him more organized.

name: Avieon Terrell
summary: Terrell is an undersized cornerback with tremendous quickness and instincts. He projects best as a nickel at the next level. He isn't as effective on the outside, where his lack of size shows up on go balls, and he'll get walled off on slants by bigger-bodied receivers.

name: Dillon Thieneman
summary: Thieneman was a versatile chess piece in Oregon’s defense. He has excellent closing speed and takes good routes. He lined up in the box, in the deep half and over the slot.

name: Jakobe Thomas
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.

name: Zakee Wheatley
summary: Wheatley is a long, athletic safety whose career arc shows steady development. He’s rangy with the ability to play over the top or close quickly to limit run-after-catch. Physicality near the line is average, but he competes and stays in the fight.

name: Collin Wright
summary: No detailed scouting report currently available.
